record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk (deeds, mortgages, liens -- one office per county; some counties have dual courthouses with district seats)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Clerk (wills, estates, guardianships); Circuit Clerk for court-related probate matters
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (marriage licenses from territorial era; Freedmen's Bureau also recorded marriages for formerly enslaved couples 1865-1872)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1914 (state-mandated birth and death registration; compliance improved through 1920s; earlier vital events in church, cemetery, and newspaper records)

burned_county_authority: Arkansas History Commission (State Archives), Little Rock -- approximately 25-30 of Arkansas's 75 counties experienced significant record losses from courthouse fires, Civil War destruction (especially in Ozark and border counties), and natural disasters (Mississippi River flooding). The Civil War was particularly destructive in northwestern Arkansas where guerrilla warfare destroyed courthouses in strongly Unionist counties. The Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies of surviving county records and is the best starting point for burned county research.
administrative_notes: Arkansas was part of the Louisiana Purchase (1803) and organized as Arkansas Territory in 1819. Admitted as the 25th state on June 15, 1836. The state's earliest European settlement was Arkansas Post (1686, French), making it one of the oldest in the Mississippi Valley. Arkansas was a Confederate state, and the Civil War devastated its records, especially in the Ozark counties of the northwest where Unionist sentiment was strong and guerrilla warfare destroyed courthouses. The state has 75 counties, several with dual courthouse arrangements (Carroll, Clay, Craighead, Logan, Sebastian, Yell). The County Clerk handles most records (deeds, marriages, probate); the Circuit Clerk handles court records. Arkansas is a public land state -- original land titles derive from federal patents through the General Land Office (GLO). The Arkansas History Commission (State Archives) in Little Rock is the central repository for state records, microfilmed county records, and Confederate/state military records. The Southwest Arkansas Regional Archives (SARA) in Washington holds significant regional materials. FamilySearch has extensively microfilmed Arkansas county records.

core_repositories.description: The state archives and primary repository for Arkansas genealogical research. Holds microfilmed county records from most counties (deeds, marriages, probate, court records), state government records, Confederate military records, territorial papers, and extensive manuscript collections. The research room in Little Rock is open to the public. Also holds newspaper microfilm, maps, and photographs.

Regional branch of the Arkansas History Commission located in historic Washington, AR (the old Hempstead County seat and Confederate capital of Arkansas). Holds records from Hempstead, Howard, Lafayette, Little River, Miller, Nevada, Pike, Polk, and Sevier counties. Strong collections for southwest Arkansas families, including Choctaw removal records and Red River plantation documents.

core_repositories.repository: Central Arkansas Library System, Little Rock
core_repositories.description: Major genealogical and historical research center in Little Rock. Holds published genealogies, county histories, Arkansas family files, photographs, maps, and the Arkansas Democrat-Gazette newspaper archives. The Bobby L. Roberts Library of Arkansas History and Art is a premier research facility.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Arkansas county records (deeds, marriages, probate), vital records, military records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at county offices and the Arkansas History Commission. Key collections include Arkansas marriages, county deed books, and federal census records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability information, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Arkansas-specific record types, burned counties, and provides county formation and boundary change information.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Arkansas newspapers. Includes the Arkansas Gazette (founded 1819, the oldest newspaper west of the Mississippi), county weeklies, and other papers. Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, and community news.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Butler Center for Arkansas Studies / Newspapers.com
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Archives of Arkansas's major daily newspaper and its predecessors. The Butler Center holds microfilm and some digitized issues. Newspapers.com has indexed and searchable Arkansas papers from many counties.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Arkansas Freedmen's Bureau Records -- NARA / FamilySearch
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Freedmen's Bureau records for Arkansas (1865-1872) including labor contracts, marriage records, school reports, complaints, and ration records. Arkansas had a large enslaved population concentrated in the delta and river counties. These records are essential for tracing African American families in the transition from slavery to freedom. FamilySearch has digitized and indexed many of these records.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Arkansas Enslaved Persons Records -- County Probate / Estate Files
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: County Clerk offices / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Estate inventories and probate records in Arkansas's plantation counties frequently list enslaved persons by name, age, value, and sometimes family groupings. These records are particularly rich in Chicot, Phillips, Jefferson, Crittenden, and other delta counties where cotton plantations were large. Tax records also enumerate enslaved persons by slaveholder.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Cherokee and Choctaw Removal Records -- NARA / Oklahoma Historical Society
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Cherokee removal (Trail of Tears) routes crossed northwest Arkansas through Washington, Benton, and Crawford counties. Choctaw removal passed through southwest Arkansas. The Quapaw people had their homeland in eastern Arkansas along the Arkansas and Mississippi Rivers before removal to Indian Territory. NARA holds treaty records, removal rolls, and Indian agency records. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds tribal records for the Five Civilized Tribes.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA / Arkansas History Commission / Quapaw Nation
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Quapaw people inhabited eastern Arkansas, particularly along the Arkansas River near Arkansas Post. Quapaw treaties (1818, 1824, 1833) ceded their lands. Records at NARA include treaty documents, annuity rolls, and agency records. The Arkansas History Commission holds some related territorial records.

military_records.source_name: Arkansas Civil War Records -- Arkansas History Commission / NARA
military_records.repository: Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock / NARA
military_records.description: Arkansas was a Confederate state but many Ozark county residents served in the Union army. The Arkansas History Commission holds Confederate service records, muster rolls, and state pension applications for Confederate veterans and widows. NARA holds Union service records and federal pension files. The state's divided loyalties make checking BOTH Union and Confederate records essential.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Arkansas
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Arkansas soldiers, both Union and Confederate. Arkansas contributed troops to both sides, with strong Union support in the Ozark counties of the northwest.

land_and_property.source_name: Arkansas Federal Land Patents -- BLM / GLO
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Arkansas is a public land state -- original land titles derive from federal patents issued through the General Land Office (GLO). The BLM website provides free access to searchable patent records by county, name, and date. These records document the initial transfer of federal land to private ownership. Donation land claims, homesteads, cash entries, and military bounty land warrants are all included.

land_and_property.source_name: Arkansas County Deed Records -- County Clerks / Arkansas History Commission
land_and_property.repository: County Clerk offices / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
land_and_property.description: County Clerk offices hold deed books recording all property conveyances after the initial federal patent. The Arkansas History Commission has microfilm copies of deed books from many counties. Some burned counties have gaps in their deed records due to courthouse fires and Civil War destruction.

vital_records.source_name: Arkansas Vital Records -- Department of Health / Arkansas History Commission
vital_records.repository: Arkansas Department of Health / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
vital_records.description: State-mandated birth and death registration began in 1914 but compliance was incomplete until the 1920s. Marriage records are held by County Clerks from the territorial era. Pre-1914 vital events must be reconstructed from church records, cemetery records, newspaper notices, and census records. Historical vital records at the Arkansas History Commission.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Various local congregations / Arkansas History Commission / University archives
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are critical for pre-civil registration vital events in Arkansas. Baptist and Methodist churches were the dominant denominations, especially in the Ozarks and rural areas. Catholic records exist in the Arkansas Post area (French colonial heritage) and in Fort Smith. Presbyterian and Church of Christ records supplement in some areas. The Arkansas History Commission holds some collected church records on microfilm.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Arkansas. Coverage varies by county. The Arkansas Gravestone Project and local genealogical societies have published cemetery transcription volumes. Rural family burial grounds, church cemeteries, and municipal cemeteries are included.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing the Arkansas Family Historian journal. Maintains research guides, surname files, and links to county-level societies. Hosts an annual conference and provides research assistance.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 75 Arkansas counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, county histories, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Arkansas has had several extinct or reorganized counties. Lovely County (1827-1828) was dissolved when the territory it occupied was ceded to the Cherokee Nation. The original Miller County (1820-1838) was abolished when the Republic of Texas claimed the territory; it was re-formed in 1874 with different boundaries. Old Hempstead County was the parent of many southwest Arkansas counties and was much larger than the present county. Researchers should be aware that many early Arkansas counties were formed from Creek and Cherokee cession lands, and territorial boundaries were fluid until statehood in 1836. Dorsey County (1873) was renamed Cleveland County in 1885. Clayton County (1873) was renamed Clay County in 1875.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Arkansas Naturalization Records -- Circuit Courts / NARA
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: County Circuit Clerk offices / NARA Fort Worth
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records for Arkansas were filed in Circuit Courts and the US District Court for the Eastern and Western Districts of Arkansas. Federal court records at NARA Fort Worth. Arkansas had relatively little foreign immigration compared to northern states, but Logan County (coal mining) and some delta counties attracted Italian, German, and Eastern European immigrants. Fort Smith's federal court handled some naturalizations for Indian Territory residents.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Arkansas has a high number of burned counties due to Civil War destruction and courthouse fires. The Ozark counties of northwestern Arkansas were particularly affected by guerrilla warfare, with courthouses in strongly Unionist counties targeted by Confederate guerrillas. Eastern delta counties suffered from occasional fires and Mississippi River flooding. The Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies of surviving county records and is the primary resource for burned county research. Federal land patent records (GLO) at the BLM website can help reconstruct destroyed county land records.
